mission.title: Install and Configure Valkey
mission.description: Valkey is a high-performance distributed key-value database optimized for caching and real-time workloads. It is suitable for applications requiring fast data access and supports a variety of data structures. Key trade-offs include the need for careful configuration to optimize performance and resource usage.

steps.title: Add the Valkey Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Valkey Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration, allowing you to install Valkey easily.

```bash

helm repo add valkey https://github.com/valkey-io/valkey/releases/download/v9.0.3

helm repo update

```

> This adds the repository for the latest release.
> Updates your local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install Valkey CRDs
steps.description:
This step installs the Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) required for Valkey to function properly in your Kubernetes cluster.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://github.com/valkey-io/valkey/releases/download/v9.0.3/valkey-crds.yaml

```

> Ensure CRDs are installed before deploying the application.

steps.title: Install Valkey using Helm
steps.description:
This command installs Valkey in the specified namespace with the desired version.

```bash

helm install valkey valkey/valkey --namespace valkey --create-namespace --version 9.0.3

```

> Replace 'valkey' with your desired release name.

steps.title: Create an example Valkey Custom Resource
steps.description:
This step creates an example Custom Resource (CR) for Valkey to manage its instances.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: valkey.io/v1alpha1
kind: Valkey
metadata:
  name: example-valkey
spec:
  replicas: 3
EOF

```

> This creates a Valkey instance with 3 replicas.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: valkey.io/v1alpha1
kind: Valkey
metadata:
  name: example-valkey
spec:
  replicas: 3
EOF

steps.title: Verify Valkey is running
steps.description:
This command checks the status of the Valkey deployment to ensure it is running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n valkey

```

> You should see the Valkey pods in a Running state.

uninstall.title: Uninstall Valkey
uninstall.description:
This step removes the Valkey deployment and associated resources. Be aware that this will result in data loss unless backups are taken.

```bash

helm uninstall valkey --namespace valkey

kubectl delete namespace valkey

```

> This command removes the Valkey release.
> This command deletes the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade Valkey
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you have a backup of your data. This process includes draining the pods, upgrading, and verifying the installation.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n valkey -o name | xargs -I {} kubectl drain {} --ignore-daemonsets --delete-local-data

helm upgrade valkey valkey/valkey --namespace valkey --version 9.0.3

kubectl get pods -n valkey

```

> Drains the pods to prepare for upgrade.
> Upgrades Valkey to the specified version.
> Verify that the new pods are running.
commands: kubectl get pods -n valkey -o name | xargs -I {} kubectl drain {} --ignore-daemonsets --delete-local-data

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to connect to Valkey server'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if the Valkey server is not running or is misconfigured.

**Fix:**
Check the status of the Valkey pods using 'kubectl get pods -n valkey' and review logs with 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n valkey'.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Valkey CRD not found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the Custom Resource Definitions were not installed properly.

**Fix:**
Reapply the CRDs with 'kubectl apply -f https://github.com/valkey-io/valkey/releases/download/v9.0.3/valkey-crds.yaml'.

troubleshooting.title: Pods are in 'CrashLoopBackOff' state
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This can happen due to misconfiguration or insufficient resources.

**Fix:**
Check the pod logs for errors and ensure your cluster has enough resources allocated.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Insufficient memory'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The Valkey pods may be requesting more memory than is available on the nodes.

**Fix:**
Adjust the resource requests in the Valkey deployment configuration.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for new data structures and improved performance for existing operations.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: No specific migration steps required for this version.

resolution.summary: You should see the Valkey pods in a Running state when executing 'kubectl get pods -n valkey'.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the required resources.
